My ASCAP statements seem really low for the number of streams I'm getting. How do I know if I'm being paid correctly? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

PRO royalty discrepancies are extremely common — and most go unnoticed because artists don't know how to audit. Here's your detective playbook:

Common reasons ASCAP payments are too low: (1) Incomplete work registration — your song isn't properly registered with ASCAP, so royalties generated by it go to the 'unmatched' pool. Check: log into ASCAP's ACE database (ascap.com/repertory) and search for each of your song titles. Verify: correct title, all writers listed, correct percentages, your IPI number attached. (2) Missing or incorrect ISRC/ISWC links — ASCAP needs these identifiers to match streaming data to your songs. If the ISRC on your distribution doesn't match what ASCAP has, royalties may go uncollected. (3) Co-writer registration issues — if your co-writer is with BMI but didn't register their share, the full song may be partially unmatched. Both writers' PROs need the work registered. (4) International performances not flowing back — ASCAP has reciprocal agreements with international PROs, but these can take 12-24 months to process.

How to audit: (1) Download your ASCAP statement detail (CSV/PDF). (2) Cross-reference with your Spotify for Artists data — identify tracks with significant streams that DON'T appear on your ASCAP statement. (3) For each missing track, verify registration in the ASCAP repertory. (4) Contact ASCAP Member Services with specific examples: 'Track [title], ISRC [code], had X streams in Q1 2026 per Spotify for Artists but does not appear on my statement dated [date].'

Expected pay rate from ASCAP for streaming: ASCAP's per-stream performance royalty rate varies but is typically $0.002-$0.004 per stream for the songwriter's share. If your track had 500,000 streams and your ASCAP payment for that track is under $300, something may be wrong.

Preventive measures: (1) Register every song with your PRO within 2 weeks of release. (2) Include ISRC codes in the registration. (3) Verify co-writers have registered their shares with their respective PROs. (4) Check the ACE/BMI repertory databases quarterly to ensure your catalog is properly reflected. (5) Consider using Songtrust or TuneCore Publishing to handle registrations globally.

ASCAP pays quarterly. There's an inherent 6-9 month delay between when a stream occurs and when you receive the royalty payment. Factor this into your analysis.

What is The MLC and why does everyone say I need to register with them? Is it like ASCAP or BMI? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

The MLC (Mechanical Licensing Collective) is NOT the same as ASCAP or BMI, and registering with it is just as important. Here's the difference:

ASCAP/BMI/SESAC = Performance Rights Organizations (PROs): They collect PERFORMANCE royalties — money generated when your song is performed publicly (radio play, live concert venues, businesses playing music, streaming platforms). You register as a songwriter.

The MLC = Mechanical Licensing Collective: They collect MECHANICAL royalties from interactive streaming (Spotify, Apple Music, Amazon Music, YouTube Music) in the US. These are royalties owed to songwriters/publishers for the reproduction of compositions. DSPs pay a blanket license fee to The MLC, which then distributes to registered songwriters.

Why you MUST register: If you're not registered with The MLC, your streaming mechanical royalties go into an 'unmatched' pool. After 3 years, unmatched royalties are distributed to OTHER songwriters/publishers based on their market share. Translation: major publishers like Sony, Universal, and Warner collect YOUR unclaimed money.

How to register (free): Go to themlc.com. Create a songwriter account. Register all of your songs with complete metadata (title, ISRC, ISWC if available, co-writer info). Link your PRO membership (ASCAP/BMI IPI number).

Common misconception: 'My distributor handles this.' WRONG. Your distributor collects your MASTER recording royalties from streaming. The MLC collects your COMPOSITION/PUBLISHING mechanical royalties. These are two different income streams paid to two different entities.

How much money: Mechanical royalties from streaming are typically 20-30% of total streaming income for the composition side. If your music earns $10,000/year in master royalties from your distributor, you likely have $2,000-$3,000 in mechanical royalties owed through The MLC.

Quick registration stack for a self-published songwriter: (1) PRO (ASCAP or BMI) — for performance royalties. (2) The MLC — for US streaming mechanical royalties. (3) SoundExchange — for non-interactive streaming/radio royalties. (4) Your distributor — for master recording royalties. All four are separate. All four are necessary. Most are free to register.

I need to release my song TOMORROW. Can we just skip the PRO registration and do it later? I don't want to delay the release. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Unknown","genre":"Pop","monthlyListeners":0,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

I understand the urgency, but let me be clear about the risks and give you a practical path forward.

Can You Release Without PRO Registration? Technically, yes — your distributor will deliver the song regardless of PRO registration status. The song will go live on streaming platforms.

But Here Is What Happens Without Registration:
- Performance royalties from Day 1 streams may not be matched to you
- Mechanical royalties via the MLC will not flow to you
- Your composition has no ISWC, making global matching harder
- If the song goes viral before registration, royalties could end up in the Black Box

The Practical Solution:
You CAN release tomorrow AND register simultaneously. PRO registration is not a prerequisite for distribution — they are parallel processes.

1. Release the song through your distributor today
2. Register the song with your PRO TODAY (online registration takes 15 minutes)
3. Register with the MLC TODAY (also done online)
4. PRO registration is retroactive — it will capture royalties from Day 1 as long as you register within a reasonable timeframe (ideally within the first month)

What I Cannot Recommend:
Skipping registration entirely and 'doing it later' often means it never gets done. I have seen artists with catalogs of 50+ songs and fewer than 10 properly registered. That is thousands of dollars in lost royalties.

Let me help you register right now — it takes 15 minutes per song and protects your revenue from Day 1.

What's the difference between ASCAP, BMI, and SESAC? Which one should I join? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"pop"}}]

A PRO (Performing Rights Organization) collects performance royalties when your music is played publicly — radio, streaming, TV, live venues, restaurants, etc. You MUST join one.

**PRO Comparison:**

| Feature | ASCAP | BMI | SESAC |
|---------|-------|-----|-------|
| Membership cost | $50 (one-time) | Free | Invitation only |
| Payment frequency | Quarterly | Quarterly | Monthly |
| Payment speed | 6-9 months after performance | 5-7 months | 3-4 months |
| Notable members | Beyoncé, Stevie Wonder | Taylor Swift, Eminem | Adele, Bob Dylan |
| Online tools | Good portal | Good portal | Excellent portal |
| Registration ease | Easy, online | Easy, online | Must be accepted |
| Advances | For established members | For established members | Available earlier |

**My Recommendation:**

*For most independent artists:*
→ **BMI** — Free to join, no upfront cost, good digital tracking

*If you have startup budget:*
→ **ASCAP** — $50 one-time, slightly faster payment processing recently

*If you're established:*
→ **SESAC** — Invitation only, but fastest payments and proactive about placements

**Critical: Register as BOTH Writer AND Publisher**
1. Join as a songwriter (writer share = 50% of performance royalties)
2. Create a publishing entity and register as a publisher (publisher share = 50%)
3. If you don't register as your own publisher, the publisher share sits UNCOLLECTED
4. This is literally leaving 50% of your performance royalties on the table

**Registration Checklist:**
- [ ] Join PRO as songwriter
- [ ] Create publishing entity (LLC or DBA — just a name is fine to start)
- [ ] Register with same PRO as publisher
- [ ] Register ALL existing songs
- [ ] Get your IPI/CAE number (assigned upon registration)

A publishing company reached out and wants to sign me. Should I do it? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging"}}]

If your goal is to stay independent, the short answer is: you don't need to sign with a publisher to collect your publishing income. Here's what you can do yourself right now at zero cost:

1. **Join a PRO** — ASCAP or BMI (both free to join; ASCAP has a one-time $50 writer registration). They collect performance royalties every time your music is streamed, broadcast, or performed publicly.
2. **Register with the MLC** (Mechanical Licensing Collective) — Free. They collect mechanical royalties from streaming services under the Music Modernization Act. If you're not registered, that money sits in a pool and eventually gets distributed to other writers.
3. **Set up a publishing admin account** — Services like Songtrust ($75 one-time), DistroKid Publishing, or CD Baby Pro register your songs internationally and collect from sub-publishers in countries where you'd otherwise miss royalties. Cost is minimal, no ownership given up.

That setup collects the same royalties a traditional publisher would collect — and you keep 100% of your publishing rights.

Where a publisher deal makes sense: if they're offering a meaningful cash advance you need for recording, they have specific sync relationships that will get your music placed, or they can open co-writing sessions with established artists you couldn't access otherwise. But those are concrete, specific benefits — not a vague promise of 'development.'

Bottom line: self-publish first. Build leverage. Sign a publishing deal only when you know exactly what you're getting in exchange for the ownership you're giving up.

I'm co-writing with someone who's signed to Sony Music Publishing. They wrote the chorus, I wrote the verses and produced the beat. How does the publishing split work when one of us is signed and the other isn't? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"pop"}}]

This is where it gets complicated and where independent artists often get shortchanged. Here's the full picture:

**The basic split:**
You and your co-writer need to agree on the ownership percentages BEFORE the song is released. A common split for your scenario: if they wrote the topline melody/chorus lyrics and you wrote the verses + produced, you might agree on 50/50 or 60/40 in your favor (production contribution counts). There is no 'standard' — it's whatever you both agree to. Get it in writing on a split sheet. Non-negotiable.

**The complication — Sony's claim:**
Your co-writer is signed to Sony Music Publishing. That means Sony owns or administers their share of the publishing. If you agree on a 50/50 split:
- Your co-writer's 50% belongs to Sony Music Publishing (their publisher's share) and your co-writer (their writer's share). The exact split depends on their deal — in a co-pub, they keep 75% of their half (37.5% total) and Sony gets 25% of their half (12.5% total).
- YOUR 50% belongs entirely to you — both writer's and publisher's share — because you're self-published.

**Chain of title:**
Sony will likely reach out (or your co-writer will loop them in) to register the song. They'll want:
- Your legal name and IPI number
- Your publishing entity name (or self-published designation)
- Confirmation of the agreed split percentage
- A signed split sheet

**Protect yourself:**
1. SIGN THE SPLIT SHEET FIRST. Before the song is released. Before it's mixed. Before Sony gets involved. The split sheet is your proof of ownership.
2. Register YOUR share yourself — with your PRO, with the MLC, and if applicable, with the Copyright Office. Don't rely on Sony to register your portion accurately.
3. Sony may propose a 'controlled composition' clause or try to administer the entire song. DO NOT let them administer your share unless you explicitly choose to. You keep your share independent.
4. Make sure the split sheet specifies: 'Each party administers their own share separately.' This prevents Sony from claiming administrative rights over your percentage.

I need a publishing administrator to collect my royalties worldwide. What are my options and which is best for indie artists? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Ren","genre":"Pop/R&B","monthlyListeners":100000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Ren, choosing the right publishing administrator is crucial. Here is a comprehensive comparison of the major options for indie artists in 2026.

What a Publishing Admin Does:
- Registers your songs globally (100+ territories via CWR)
- Collects BOTH performance AND mechanical royalties worldwide
- Handles YouTube Content ID for composition claims
- Files necessary licenses and paperwork
- You RETAIN 100% of your copyright (they are an admin, not a publisher)

Comparison:

Songtrust:
- Commission: 15% of collected royalties
- Annual Fee: None
- Coverage: 60+ collection societies worldwide
- Strengths: Purpose-built for publishing admin, transparent dashboard, strong international coverage
- Best for: Artists with growing international streams

CD Baby Pro Publishing:
- Commission: 15% of publishing royalties
- Annual Fee: Included with distribution (no separate fee)
- Coverage: 50+ territories
- Strengths: Integrated with distribution, one-stop shop
- Best for: CD Baby distribution users who want simplicity

TuneCore Publishing:
- Commission: 15% of publishing royalties
- Annual Fee: None (included with TuneCore distribution subscription)
- Coverage: 60+ societies
- Strengths: Integrated with TuneCore distribution
- Best for: TuneCore distribution users

Sentric Music:
- Commission: 20% for first 2 years, then negotiable
- Annual Fee: None
- Coverage: 60+ territories
- Strengths: Strong in Europe (UK-based), sync pitching included
- Best for: Artists with heavy European audiences

Downtown / Songtrust (Parent Company):
- Commission: 10-15% negotiable for larger catalogs
- Coverage: Full global network
- Strengths: Full-service administration with major label infrastructure
- Best for: Artists approaching mid-level with 200K+ monthly listeners

My Recommendation for You:
At 100K monthly listeners in Pop/R&B, Songtrust at 15% is the strongest standalone option — purpose-built, transparent, and with robust global coverage. If you are already with CD Baby or TuneCore for distribution, using their integrated publishing admin reduces complexity.

I've heard there are 'black box' royalties sitting unclaimed. How does that work and how do I make sure I'm not losing money to it? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"indie"}}]

Black box royalties are real, significant, and disproportionately affect independent artists. Here's exactly what they are and how to stop losing to them.

**What black box royalties are:**
When a streaming platform or broadcaster generates a royalty payment but can't match it to a specific rights holder — because the metadata is incomplete, the songwriter isn't registered, or the publishing information is missing — that money goes into an 'unmatched' or 'black box' pool. After a holding period (typically 3-5 years depending on the society), the money gets redistributed based on market share. Translation: your unclaimed royalties end up going to Universal, Sony, and Warner because they have the largest market share.

**How much money we're talking about:** Estimates vary, but the MLC (Mechanical Licensing Collective) in the US alone held $424 million in unmatched mechanical royalties as of early 2025. Globally, the number is likely $2-3 billion+ across all collection societies.

**How to make sure you're not losing to it:**
1. **Register with the MLC (themlc.com)** — free. They collect mechanical royalties from interactive streaming in the US. If you self-published and never registered, your mechanicals are in their black box right now.
2. **Register with your PRO as BOTH a writer AND publisher** — if you only registered as a writer, you're missing the publisher's share of performance royalties (that's 50% of your performance income).
3. **Get an IPI number** — this is your international publishing identifier. ASCAP/BMI assign one when you register. Make sure every song you distribute includes your IPI in the publishing metadata.
4. **Complete every metadata field** when distributing: songwriter legal name (consistent spelling), publisher name, IPI number, ISWC (if available). Every blank field is another chance for your royalty to go unmatched.
5. **Use a service like Songtrust or PubInTech** to register your catalog across 40+ international collection societies — many countries have their own pools of unmatched royalties.

The system is designed such that the least organized rights holders lose the most money. As an indie, that's you — unless you're meticulous about registration. Be meticulous.

Should I sign a publishing deal? A publisher reached out and wants to sign me. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"label-deal","stage":"developing","genre":"country"}}]

With your goal of landing a label deal, a publishing deal plays differently — it can actually be a strategic step, but you need to negotiate it correctly.

Major labels and their A&R teams pay attention to who's signed where. A publishing deal with a reputable publisher (ole, Sony Music Publishing, Universal Music Publishing, peermusic) signals legitimate industry validation and can open doors to co-writing sessions with established writers — which is one of the primary career accelerators in country music.

**What to negotiate given your goal:**

Co-publishing (50/50 split) is standard at your stage. Don't accept less than 50% of your publisher's share. The full structure should be: you keep 100% of your writer's share + 50% of the publisher's share = you keep 75% of total publishing.

Key terms to lock in:
- Creative approval: you must approve any cut of your songs before it releases
- Minimum commitment: they must place X songs per year or release you from exclusivity
- Reversion: if they don't deliver, your rights come back
- Sync pipeline: get specifics on what placements and co-writes they actively generate in your genre
- Advance: $25k+ at a developing stage in country is reasonable; less than that, question their commitment

**The advocate position:** A publishing deal isn't the goal — it's a tool. If this publisher has a genuine pipeline to the writers and A&R contacts who can accelerate your label path, negotiate hard and take it. If they're offering $5k and vague promises, walk. Your publishing is leverage — use it strategically, don't give it away.

What's an IPI number and do I need one? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

An **IPI (Interested Party Information)** number is your unique global identifier as a songwriter/publisher. Think of it as your Social Security Number for music rights.

**You get one automatically** when you join a PRO (ASCAP, BMI, SESAC). It's used to identify you across all international collecting societies.

**Why it matters:** Without it, your international royalties can't be matched to you — they become Black Box money.

Are you registered with a PRO? If not, that's the first step.

My publishing administrator mentioned something called CWR files. What are those and why do they matter? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Blake","genre":"Pop","monthlyListeners":100000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Blake, CWR is the backbone of international music publishing data exchange. Here is what you need to know.

What Is CWR?
CWR stands for Common Works Registration — a standardized electronic format developed by CISAC (the international confederation of collecting societies) for registering musical works with collection societies worldwide. Think of it as the DDEX equivalent for publishing.

Why It Matters:
Before CWR, publishers had to register songs individually with each society in each territory — a manual, error-prone process. CWR allows a single standardized file to be sent to dozens of societies simultaneously.

A CWR File Contains:
1. Work identification: title, ISWC (if assigned), alternative titles
2. Writer information: legal names, IPI numbers, PRO affiliations, ownership percentages
3. Publisher information: legal name, IPI number, territory-specific sub-publisher chains
4. Territory-specific rights: which publisher controls rights in which territory
5. Performance, mechanical, and synchronization shares

Who Creates CWR Files?
- Major publishers: have in-house systems to generate CWR
- Publishing administrators (Songtrust, CD Baby, Downtown): generate CWR on your behalf as part of their service
- Self-published: you would need specialized software to generate CWR (most independent songwriters use an admin service instead)

The Registration Chain:
1. Your publishing administrator generates a CWR file containing your works
2. They submit it to the relevant collection societies
3. Each society processes the registration and adds the work to their repertoire
4. Society acknowledges registration (CWR acknowledgment file)
5. When your music is used in that territory, the society collects and distributes royalties

Common CWR Issues:
- Writer name mismatches between societies (John Smith vs J. Smith)
- Missing or incorrect IPI numbers
- Conflicting ownership claims from different publishers registering the same work
- These conflicts create 'blackout' periods where royalties are held until resolved

At your level, your publishing admin should be handling CWR registration. If they are not registering internationally, you are leaving money on the table.

I keep getting offers from publishing companies. What's the difference between a publishing admin deal and a full publishing deal? Should I sign one? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

Three options, from least to most commitment:

1. Self-Publishing (DIY): You register as your own publisher, handle all admin yourself. PRO registration, MLC registration, sync pitching (if any), international collection (limited without sub-publishers). Cost: $0. You keep 100%. Downside: you miss international royalties and sync opportunities.

2. Publishing Admin Deal: A company handles all the paperwork — PRO registration, mechanical collection, international sub-publishing, royalty tracking — for a commission (typically 10-20%). You retain 100% copyright ownership. Term is usually 1-3 years. No advance. Examples: Songtrust (15%), Sentric (20% first term), CD Baby Pro (15%), TuneCore Publishing. Best for: most indie artists. You get global collection without giving up ownership.

3. Traditional/Co-Publishing Deal: A publisher takes 50% of your publisher's share (so they get 25% of total publishing, you keep 75%). In exchange: upfront advance ($5k-$500k+ depending on your catalog), active sync pitching, co-writing opportunities, creative development. You typically assign copyright to the publisher for 10-15+ years or life of copyright. Best for: artists who need capital or want active creative development.

My recommendation for most indie artists in 2026: Start with a publishing admin deal (Songtrust or Sentric). You'll immediately start collecting 15-40% more royalties from international and mechanical sources without giving up any ownership. If a traditional publisher offers a meaningful advance and you understand the ownership implications, that's a separate conversation.

Red flags in publishing offers: life-of-copyright terms, first-refusal on future works, controlled composition clauses, key-person clauses that don't name the A&R you'd be working with.

An artist in Brazil wants to translate my song lyrics into Portuguese and record their own version. What are the publishing implications? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Lara","genre":"Pop","monthlyListeners":120000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Lara, a lyric translation creates a 'derivative work' — a new version of your original composition. Here is how the publishing rights work.

What Is a Derivative Work?
A derivative work is a new creative work based on an existing copyrighted work. Translations, adaptations, arrangements, and dramatizations are all derivative works. The original copyright owner (you) must authorize the creation of any derivative work.

Publishing Implications of a Translation:

1. Your Permission Is Required: The Brazilian artist cannot translate and release without your authorization. As the copyright owner, you have the exclusive right to prepare derivative works.

2. The Translator May Earn Co-Writer Credit: If the translator creates new lyrical content (not just a literal word-for-word translation), they may be entitled to a share of the derivative work's publishing. Common splits: translator gets 10-25% of the new version's publishing, you retain 75-90%.

3. A Literal Translation: If it is a direct, faithful translation with minimal creative interpretation, the translator typically receives no publishing share — they are acting as a translator, not a songwriter.

4. Two Registrations Needed: the original version (registered with your PRO, 100% your publishing) and the Portuguese version (registered as a derivative work, with split per your agreement).

5. Mechanical License: The Brazilian artist needs a mechanical license to record and distribute the derivative version. Since you own the publishing, you can grant this directly.

How to Structure the Deal:
1. Written license agreement specifying: permission to translate, approved territories (Brazil only? All Portuguese-speaking territories?), revenue split, credit format, duration of the license, whether they can create further derivatives.

2. ISWC: The translated version should receive its own ISWC, distinct from the original.

3. PRO Registration: Both versions registered accurately, with the Portuguese version linked to your original as a derivative.

This could be a great opportunity to expand your audience in Brazil — Spotify's third-largest market globally.

I co-wrote a song with two other people. How do we split the royalties fairly? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"R&B"}}]

The #1 rule in publishing: **AGREE ON SPLITS BEFORE YOU LEAVE THE ROOM.** This avoids 90% of music industry disputes.

**The Split Sheet (What It Is):**
A written agreement documenting each contributor's ownership percentage of a song's composition. It's a legally binding document.

**What to Include:**
- Song title and working title (if different)
- Date of creation
- Names of ALL contributors
- Percentage ownership for each contributor
- PRO affiliation for each contributor (ASCAP, BMI, SESAC)
- IPI/CAE number for each contributor
- Publisher information (if any contributor has a publishing deal)
- Signatures from ALL parties

**Common Split Approaches:**

| Method | How It Works | When to Use |
|--------|------------|-------------|
| Equal split | 33.3% / 33.3% / 33.3% | All contributed equally, simplest |
| Role-based | Lyrics writer gets X%, melody gets Y%, production gets Z% | Clear role separation |
| Contribution-based | Negotiated per contribution level | Complex sessions, varying input |

**Best Practices:**
1. **Sign BEFORE releasing.** No exceptions.
2. **Include producers.** If the producer contributed to the composition (melody, chord changes, arrangement ideas), they deserve a publishing split. Production work alone (mixing, engineering) is NOT a publishing contribution.
3. **Samples count.** If you sampled someone, their split comes off the top before your team splits.
4. **Use a template.** Free split sheet templates from ASCAP, BMI, or Songtrust.
5. **Register independently.** Each co-writer registers the song with their own PRO, listing ALL writers and their splits.

**The Conversation Script:**
'Before we finish, let's get the splits down. I think [X] makes sense because [reasoning]. What do you think?'

Have this conversation while the creative energy is good — not months later when money is involved.

Someone told me I'm missing out on 'neighboring rights' royalties. What are those? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"R&B"}}]

Neighboring rights are royalties paid to PERFORMERS and RECORDING OWNERS when their recorded music is played publicly. They're separate from and IN ADDITION TO your publishing/composition royalties.

**The Royalty Landscape:**

| Royalty Type | Who Gets Paid | Who Collects | Source |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|--------|
| Performance (composition) | Songwriter | PRO (ASCAP/BMI) | Radio, TV, venues |
| Mechanical (composition) | Songwriter | MLC / Publisher | Streaming, physical sales |
| **Neighboring Rights (recording)** | **Performer + Master Owner** | **SoundExchange / CMO** | **Digital radio, public performance** |
| Master royalties | Master owner | Distributor | Streaming, downloads |

**SoundExchange (US):**
- Collects digital performance royalties from Pandora, SiriusXM, and non-interactive streaming
- Splits: 45% featured artist, 5% session musicians (AFM/SAG-AFTRA fund), 50% master owner
- As an independent artist who owns your masters: you receive 95% (45% + 50%)
- **Free to register** — sign up at soundexchange.com
- Many independent artists have UNCOLLECTED royalties sitting there right now

**International Neighboring Rights:**
- Outside the US, neighboring rights also apply to terrestrial radio and public venues
- CMOs (Collective Management Organizations) collect in each territory:
  - UK: PPL
  - Germany: GVL
  - France: SCPP/SPPF
  - Japan: CPRA
- To collect internationally, register with a neighboring rights administrator:
  - Kobalt Neighboring Rights
  - Downtown Music
  - Songtrust

**Action Items:**
1. Register with SoundExchange immediately (free money you're missing)
2. Register ALL recordings with ISRCs
3. Consider a neighboring rights administrator for international collection
4. Check if you have uncollected royalties — SoundExchange has a search tool

I got invited to a songwriting camp. There will be 20 writers I've never met. How do I protect my publishing and make sure I get credit? [Context: {"currentModule":"publishing","hasProject":true}]

Songwriting camps are amazing opportunities — they've produced massive hits. But they're also where publishing disputes are born if you don't protect yourself. Here's the protocol:

Before the camp: (1) Understand the camp's default split policy. Many camps have a 'room split' rule: everyone in the room when a song is written gets equal credit, regardless of contribution. A song written by 4 people = 25% each. Ask the camp organizer for their policy in writing. (2) Know your PRO info cold: your PRO (ASCAP/BMI), your IPI/CAE number, your legal name, and your publisher entity name (even if it's just you). (3) Bring pre-existing ideas/demos if you want, but know that once you bring something INTO a co-write, others may claim contribution to the final version.

During the session: (1) At the start of each session, agree verbally on the split arrangement: equal shares, or contribution-based. (2) Document who's in the room. Take photos, note names. (3) If you bring a pre-existing melody or lyric into the room that becomes part of the song, STATE IT CLEARLY: 'I'm bringing this chorus melody I wrote last week — I'd like to maintain my original contribution in the split.' (4) Don't be the person who sits on the couch playing their phone and then claims 25% because they were 'in the room.'

After the session: (1) SIGN A SPLIT SHEET that day — not 'later,' not 'when we get a deal.' NOW. The camp should provide forms. If they don't, bring your own. (2) The split sheet must include: song title (even a working title), all writers' legal names, PRO affiliations, IPI numbers, percentage splits, publisher information, date and signatures. (3) Take a photo of the signed split sheet immediately.

Post-camp: Register the song with your PRO within 2 weeks. Register with The MLC. Share the split sheet with any other writers' publishers who request it.

Protecting your pre-existing work: If you bring a finished or near-finished demo that gets 'improved' at the camp, negotiate for a larger share (40-50%). Alternatively, license it to the co-writers rather than splitting publishing.

Politics: Be generous but not foolish. Equal splits build relationships. Nickel-and-diming co-writers over 5% differences will earn you a reputation as difficult. But don't give up credit you've earned — that compounds over your career.

How does YouTube Content ID work for publishing? I know my distributor handles the master side, but what about the composition? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Fern","genre":"Indie","monthlyListeners":75000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"publishing"}]

Fern, great question. YouTube Content ID has TWO separate layers of claims, and most independent artists only have one set up.

Master Recording Content ID (Sound Recording):
Your distributor (DistroKid, TuneCore, CD Baby) handles this. They register your specific audio recording so when anyone uploads a video using your exact recording, a Sound Recording claim is automatically placed. Revenue goes to you via your distributor.

Composition Content ID (Musical Composition):
This is the PUBLISHING side. When someone creates a cover, uses your melody, or re-records your song, the master Content ID will NOT catch it — because it is a different recording. Composition Content ID catches these by matching the underlying musical work (melody, chords, lyrics).

Who Handles Composition Content ID?
- If you have a publishing administrator (Songtrust, Downtown, CD Baby Pro): they register your compositions for Content ID and collect the publishing revenue
- If you have a publisher: they handle this
- If you are fully self-published with NO admin: this revenue may be uncollected

Why This Matters:
Imagine someone posts a guitar cover of your song that gets 2 million views. Your distributor's master Content ID will NOT trigger (different recording). But composition Content ID WILL trigger and monetize that video — with revenue going to you as the songwriter.

At 75K monthly listeners, covers and UGC using your compositions on YouTube could generate $500-$2,000/year in composition Content ID revenue. If you do not have composition Content ID active, that money goes uncollected.

Action Items:
1. Confirm with your distributor whether they handle composition Content ID (some only handle master)
2. If not, sign up with a publishing admin that includes YouTube composition claims
3. Check YouTube Studio for existing claims on your songs — you should see both SR (Sound Recording) and C (Composition) claims on videos using your music
